common_name: Aji Crystal Pepper (Chilean Mild Yellow Baccatum Sweet Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Aji Crystal is a Capsicum baccatum species pepper, which requires slightly higher germination temperatures than C. annuum types and benefits from longer indoor growing time before transplanting. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer harvest. The pale, translucent-looking fruits develop their characteristic crystal-clear quality when harvested at the green-yellow stage; harvesting when pale yellow-cream rather than waiting for orange-red gives the mildest, most crisp results.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun after last frost. Harvest pale yellow to cream-white fruits for mildest flavor; allow to ripen orange-red for sweeter, more developed flavor. Aji Crystal is a Chilean baccatum pepper from the Capsicum baccatum species — a pepper species distinct from the common C. annuum and characterized by the spotted calyx markings at the base of the flower, a more complex flavor profile, and a heat level that can range from mild to medium-hot. Aji Crystal is at the mild end of the baccatum spectrum, with very low heat (typically under 500 SHU) that makes it essentially a sweet pepper with baccatum's distinctive flavor complexity. The fruits are elongated (4–5 inches), thin-walled, and pale yellow to near-white at the harvest stage — almost translucent in appearance, suggesting the crystal-clear quality for which the variety is named. The flavor is crisp, bright, slightly sweet and tangy with a fruity, aromatic quality distinctive of baccatum peppers — difficult to describe but clearly different from C. annuum sweet peppers and very pleasant raw in salads, stuffed, or used fresh in Peruvian-style preparations where baccatum peppers are traditional. Aji Crystal is popular among pepper enthusiasts and chefs interested in flavors beyond the standard C. annuum palette.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Aji Crystal pepper variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Specialty pepper variety descriptions

common_name: Red Marconi Frying Pepper (Italian Open-Pollinated Sweet Roasting Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Red Marconi is an open-pollinated Italian frying pepper that can be seed-saved year to year — select the best-shaped, most productive fruits from the healthiest plants. In zone 9, transplant March–April. Harvest green for frying (sweeter and more delicate) or let ripen to full deep red for roasting — the red-ripe stage has dramatically more sweetness and vitamin C than the green stage.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 78–85°F; transplant to full sun, fertile, well-drained soil. Red Marconi is the classic Italian open-pollinated sweet frying/roasting pepper — a traditional heirloom variety that produces long (6–8 inch), moderately tapered, thick-walled, slightly ridged fruits of the Italian frying type, ripening from medium green to deep, vivid red. Unlike the Giant Marconi hybrid (AAS 1997), Red Marconi is open-pollinated, allowing seed to be saved and maintained from year to year — the preservation choice for gardeners who want a self-sustaining seed supply. The traditional Italian frying pepper form is distinctive: longer and more tapered than bell peppers, with a slightly irregular, ridged exterior and relatively thick, meaty walls. At the green stage, the flavor is mild, fresh, and subtly sweet — the standard for Italian sautéed peppers (peperoni fritti) where the pepper is sliced and cooked in olive oil until tender. At the red stage, the flavor concentrates into a sweet, rich, complex character ideal for roasting, peeling, and incorporating into antipasto, stuffed preparations, or the classic Italian peperonata. Red Marconi plants are tall (30–42 inches), productive, and require staking when loaded with fruit.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Italian frying pepper collection
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Red Marconi variety notes
Franchi Sementi (Italy) — Italian heirloom pepper documentation

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; soak seeds overnight in warm water before planting to improve germination. Blondy's compact size (3–4 feet vs. the 5–6 feet of standard okra) makes it appropriate for smaller gardens. In zone 9, plant April–June for summer and fall harvest. Harvest pods every 2–3 days when 2–4 inches long — Blondy pods stay tender longer than dark green types, remaining usable up to 5–6 inches without becoming tough and fibrous.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 12–18 inches. Harvest cream-colored pods every 2–3 days at 2–5 inches. Blondy is a 1986 All-America Selections-winning okra that earned recognition for two distinguishing traits: compact plant size and distinctive cream-white to pale chartreuse pod color. Standard okra varieties like Clemson Spineless and Emerald are tall plants (5–7 feet) that require staking and become difficult to harvest at the top as the season progresses; Blondy stays compact at 3–4 feet, keeping all pods within easy reach throughout the harvest season. The pale cream-colored pods are the most visually distinctive feature — where standard okra is dark forest green, Blondy's pods are almost white-green to cream-yellow, giving a noticeably different appearance both in the garden and on the table. Beyond the visual novelty, the pale pods have a slight cooking advantage: they do not contribute the dark green color that sometimes bleeds into dishes, making them preferable for light-colored preparations like cream soups, gumbo with light roux, or pickled okra where visual clarity is desired. The pods remain tender at larger sizes than dark-green types, providing a more forgiving harvest window. Blondy produces heavily on its compact plants, making it particularly well-suited to small gardens and raised beds.
sources: All-America Selections — Blondy okra AAS winner 1986
Burpee Seeds — Blondy okra variety notes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Okra Variety Recommendations

scientific_name: Sorghum bicolor × Sorghum bicolor var. drummondii

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; sow at 30–40 lbs/acre or broadcast and rake in for home garden use. Sorghum-sudangrass is the ideal summer cover crop for the Gulf South (zone 8–10) where cool-season covers like crimson clover or winter rye die in summer heat. Mow at 3–4 feet tall before heading to prevent seed set and stimulate regrowth; can be mowed 2–3 times per season for maximum biomass. Incorporate 2–3 weeks before planting fall crops to allow decomposition.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed after last frost at 4–6 lbs per 1,000 sq ft; mow at 3–4 feet before heading to stimulate regrowth. Incorporate or mow and compost 2–3 weeks before planting fall crops. Sorghum-sudangrass hybrid (sometimes sold as 'sudex' or 'sordan') is the most important summer annual cover crop for warm-climate gardens in zones 7–10 — the warm-season counterpart to winter rye or winter cover crop mixes that die when summer heat arrives. The hybrid is produced by crossing grain sorghum (Sorghum bicolor) with sudangrass (a variety of the same species), resulting in a vigorous, fast-growing, heat-tolerant grass that can produce 4,000–6,000 lbs of dry matter per acre in a single season. For the Gulf South garden, sorghum-sudangrass solves the challenging 'summer fallow' problem: from June through September, heat and humidity make most vegetable production difficult, and bare soil is subject to erosion, compaction, weed invasion, and nutrient leaching. A sorghum-sudangrass cover fills that window with living roots (suppressing nematodes through allelopathic compounds), dense canopy (shading out weeds), and substantial organic matter that improves soil tilth for fall planting. The roots reach 6–8 feet deep, breaking through hardpan and improving drainage in compacted soils.
sources: USDA NRCS — Sorghum-sudangrass cover crop planting guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cover Crops for Florida and Gulf South Soils
Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education (SARE) — Cover Crop Encyclopedia

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; Poinsett 76 is the standard open-pollinated slicing cucumber for the southeastern United States, developed at Clemson University specifically for warm, humid conditions where downy mildew, angular leaf spot, and other diseases that terminate cucumber plantings early are endemic. In zone 9, plant March–April for June–July harvest and again in late August for fall production. The '76' designation indicates the 1976 re-selection of the original Poinsett variety released by Clemson in the 1960s.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in hills or rows; thin to 12–18 inches. Harvest slicers at 7–8 inches for best flavor and texture. Poinsett 76 is an open-pollinated slicing cucumber developed at Clemson University and first released in the 1960s, with the '76' selection refined and released in 1976 — a vegetable breeding achievement directly targeted at the challenging growing conditions of the southeastern United States where heat, humidity, and endemic fungal diseases make many cucumber varieties unproductive. The variety carries resistance to angular leaf spot, anthracnose, downy mildew, powdery mildew, and cucumber mosaic virus — a disease resistance package that keeps plants productive through the humid Gulf South growing season when susceptible varieties fail. The fruits are classic dark green slicers: 7–9 inches long, cylindrical, dark forest green with small white spines, tender skin, crisp white flesh, and mild flavor — the traditional American slicing cucumber type at its best. Poinsett 76 is the most widely recommended open-pollinated slicing cucumber for the southeastern United States in cooperative extension publications, where it appears consistently as the standard recommendation for humid, warm climates. Seed can be saved from year to year from well-isolated plants.
sources: Clemson University Cooperative Extension — Poinsett 76 cucumber development
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Poinsett 76 variety notes
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Cucumber Variety Recommendations for the Southeast
